title: New Middle East corridors are about more than just bypassing the Strait of Hormuz
gist: The Strait of Hormuz disruption is accelerating Gulf-to-Mediterranean overland rail, pipeline, and digital corridors; the author argues IMEC must become a modular multi-route network (potentially absorbing 60 percent of pre-war Hormuz container traffic) and adds a 'compute corridor' vision positioning the Gulf as the world's third-largest compute hub.

insights.claim: The disruption of the Strait of Hormuz is propelling a surge of support for new overland trade and energy corridors connecting the Gulf to the Mediterranean, with governments fast-tracking infrastructure, streamlining customs, and exploring new rail and pipeline projects.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Chokepoint risk is triggering a durable overland infrastructure buildout; exposure to Gulf rail, logistics, and pipeline projects is a multi-year theme.
insights.locator: The disruption of the world's most significant maritime

insights.claim: Major infrastructure corridor projects can take a generation to generate returns, and the private sector is wary of investing in routes that traverse difficult political and security environments and are largely seen as backups to maritime routes.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Durable political signaling is the precondition for private capital; without it, corridor projects stall regardless of strategic logic.
insights.locator: Realizing the potential of these projects demands

insights.claim: Saudi Arabia is building up the Port of NEOM as a new logistics hub and expanding rail networks to both the Red Sea and Jordan, while the UAE and Oman are completing a new rail line boosting Oman's ports, which sit outside both the Strait of Hormuz and Bab al-Mandab.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Oman's ports outside both chokepoints become strategically valuable; Saudi and Emirati rail investment reshapes regional logistics geography.
insights.locator: A patchwork of projects are providing

insights.claim: The UAE is constructing a new pipeline that will double its ability to export oil outside the Strait of Hormuz, progressing rapidly because it does not need to cross borders and benefits from ample access to capital.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: The UAE is the near-term winner in Hormuz bypass capacity; cross-border pipeline projects for Iraq, Kuwait, Qatar, and Bahrain face far higher hurdles.
insights.locator: The urgency of the energy crisis created

insights.claim: Saudi Arabia and Turkey are reportedly exploring reviving a rail line connecting Jordan to Syria, complementing emerging World Bank support for Syrian rail and Emirati investment in Syrian ports, opening new Mediterranean access points.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Syria's reintegration via ports and rail is an emerging reconstruction and connectivity play backed by Gulf and multilateral capital.
insights.locator: Saudi Arabia and Turkey are also reportedly

insights.claim: The Middle East is already littered with the skeletons of abandoned pipelines like the Trans-Arabian Pipeline, making both governments and private investors wary of projects vulnerable to insecurity, cross-border disputes, or waning economic viability.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: History of stranded pipeline assets is why execution certainty and cross-border accountability are the binding constraints, not engineering.
insights.locator: The Middle East is already littered

insights.claim: If the Strait of Hormuz returns to pre-war status, some new pipelines would become obsolete as oil and gas exports revert to more flexible, cost-effective maritime routes.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Bypass-pipeline economics hinge on Hormuz staying disrupted; stranded-asset risk is real if the strait normalizes.
insights.locator: Uncertainty over the future of the Strait

insights.claim: Iran's insistence on asserting control over Hormuz and the example of Red Sea traffic still not rebounding after the Houthi campaign suggest commercial confidence in the strait will be difficult to restore, keeping alternative routes attractive.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Persistent higher insurance rates and confidence loss make the corridor buildout structurally sticky even after physical reopening.

insights.claim: A recent Atlantic Council report finds that by integrating new routes through Oman, Egypt, and Syria, IMEC could absorb over 60 percent of the container traffic that transited Hormuz pre-war and increase corridor capacity tenfold.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: A networked IMEC could realistically hedge most Hormuz container flow, quantifying the upside for corridor infrastructure and logistics operators.
insights.locator: First, IMEC must embrace a network approach

insights.claim: IMEC should adopt a modular approach where the sherpa process coordinates a patchwork of independently financed national and private projects and harmonizes regulation, rather than overseeing construction itself.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Value is in regulatory harmonization and digital customs, not building; that is where policy progress and trade-cost reduction will actually come from.
insights.locator: Third, IMEC signatory governments must clarify

insights.claim: The East Mediterranean Gas Forum shows a regional coordination platform can succeed even amid difficult politics when it has a strong regional champion (Egypt) and focuses on tangible multi-country projects.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: A dominant regional champion and concrete deliverables are the success template; IMEC's viability depends on replicating them.
insights.locator: The East Mediterranean Gas Forum (EMGF) provides

insights.claim: The Gulf is already teed up to become the third largest source of compute power in the world, behind the United States and China, leveraging its geography, compute investments, and cost-effective energy.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: The Gulf as the world's #3 compute hub is a major AI-infrastructure and data-center investment thesis tied to cheap energy.
insights.locator: The Gulf is already teed up

insights.claim: An effective Middle East compute corridor demands strong governance frameworks for cross-border data flows and resilience against both cyber and kinetic attacks, with Iranian attacks on Gulf data centers fueling momentum on the latter.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Physical and cyber attacks on Gulf data centers are a live risk that will drive spending on data-center hardening and security.
insights.locator: But an effective compute corridor also demands

insights.claim: The 2023 IMEC memorandum focused on hydrogen exports, electricity interconnection, and fiber-optic cables, but the author argues resilience now demands a hybrid approach combining renewables with a limited number of disciplined pipeline projects.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: The energy-corridor vision is shifting from pure clean-energy toward a hydrocarbon-plus-renewables hybrid, broadening the investable project set.
insights.locator: The 2023 IMEC Memorandum of Understanding focused
